title-group.article-title: Distal forelimb radiographic bone morphology in Thoroughbred foals during the first 10 months post‐partum. Part 1: Carpus

aff.named-content: School of Equine science and Horticulture

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceJung K. Son, Department of Veterinary BioSciences, The University of Melbourne. Email: jungs1@student.unimelb.edu.auSeungho Ryu, School of Equine Science and Horticulture, Cheju Halla University, Cheju, South Korea. Email: batmanryu@hanmail.net

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The risk of carpal injury in racehorses may be related to the morphology, yet whether carpal morphologies are set from birth or change through growth remains unclear.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To quantify carpal bone changes through growth.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: Twenty privately owned Thoroughbred foals born between January 2022 and May 2023 were radiographed bimonthly from birth to 10 months of age. Imprint training was used to take radiographs safely without chemical restraints. Fifteen individual and 11 relative angular carpal parameters were measured using ImageJ on dorsopalmar radiographs of the carpus at zero degrees of vertical and horizontal rotation. Associations with age (growth), sex and the differences between left and right limbs were analysed separately using a linear mixed effects model.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six individual carpal parameters changed with age (radial carpal joint [RCJ], Prx.dor. radial carpal [Cr], Prx.Cu, Dis.dor. third carpal [C3], Dis.pal.C3 and Dis.pal. intermediate carpal), and one was influenced by side, that is higher in the left carpus (Dis.pal.Cr). Seven relative parameters changed with age, and one relative parameter was influenced by side, that is higher in the left (Ra.met‐RCJ). The proximo‐dorsal bone surface angle of Cr and disto‐dorsal bone surface angle of C3 became flatter over time, which may be associated with the re‐direction of the load towards the sagittal carpal plane. Sex did not influence any of the carpal parameters, nor did the combined effect of age, side of the limb and sex.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Specific individual and relative angular carpal parameters changed significantly over time and some differed between the left and right limb, whereas other parameters did not change. The steeper carpal bone angles achieved proximally with the parameters that did change may improve stability by redirecting the load more medially through the carpus and the proximal and distal bones.

abstract.p: This report describes the change in specific radiographic carpal parameters in Thoroughbred foals through the first 10 months of growth. Several carpal parameters changed significantly over time, whereas variations in two parameters were explained by side (left or right). The steeper carpal bone angles achieved proximally together with the parameters that remained constant may act to improve stability by redirecting the load more medially through the carpus and to the proximal and distal bones.

p.mixed-citation: Son, J. K., De Paz, P., Kim, J., Sanaei, R., Seungho, R., Bailey, S., & Davies, H. M. S. (2024). Distal forelimb radiographic bone morphology in Thoroughbred foals during the first 10 months post‐partum. Part 1: Carpus. Veterinary Medicine and Science, e31539. 10.1002/vms3.153939018065PMC11253829

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: The carpus has been shown to be a common location of pathology and injuries causing lameness in sport horses. The carpal joint was the third highest site for damage in 248 2 and 3‐year‐old Australian Thoroughbred racehorses (Cogger et al., 2008). Further, the carpus was found to be one of the most common sites of fracture in 64,807 racing starts at Hong Kong Jockey Club (Sun et al., 2019). The most common sites of carpal chip fracture in Thoroughbred racehorses were found to be the radial carpal bone (Cr), intermediate carpal bone (Ci) and the third carpal bone (C3) (Firth et al., 1999; McIlwraith, 2022; Schneider et al., 1988; Shimozawa et al., 2001). Although the statistical significance was not reported, the incidence rate of carpal injuries has been reported to be higher in 2‐year‐olds than in 3‐year‐olds (Cogger et al., 2008). This higher incidence in younger horses suggests that either horses prone to carpal damage were removed before they reached the 3‐year‐old threshold or that the adaptive response of the carpus at the introduction of high‐intensity exercise in younger horses may be associated with early career carpal injuries.

Bones adapt and change their shape when exposed to different loading regimes (Davies et al., 1999; Firth et al., 2011). Thus, if carpal damage is associated with training at high speeds, then there may be differences in carpal bone shape and type of injuries between horses that race at slower speeds or in different gaits (Palmer, 1986; Pasquini et al., 1997). This hypothesis is supported by the results of a study by Oheida et al. (2019), which showed that the distal carpal row in Standardbreds had a greater inclination in the distomedial plane than in Thoroughbred horses. Further, previous studies showed that distal Cr chip fracture was the most prevalent carpal fracture in Thoroughbreds, whereas the proximal C3 chip fracture was more frequently found in Standardbreds (Palmer, 1986; Pasquini et al., 1997). However, as these studies were conducted with adult horses retrospectively, it is unclear whether the differences in these carpal parameters were from genetics, growth or human‐induced exercise later in life.

Radiographic evaluation allows prospective observation of the anatomical region of interest and provides repeatable and reliable measurements in the distal limb of the horse (Oheida, 2011; Walter & Davies, 2001). Oheida (2011) presented angular and proportional carpal bone parameters from dorsopalmar radiographs (DP) at zero degrees (ZDP) or within an acceptable range of rotation. Specific angular parameters were related to middle carpal joint (MCJ) fracture and early career MCJ damage in Thoroughbred and Standardbred racehorses (Oheida et al., 2016; Oheida et al., 2019). Higher mean angles between the radial carpal joint (RCJ) and radial facet of C3 as well as between the radial metaphysis and radial facet of C3 were found among experienced racehorses free from MCJ fractures compared to horses with early career MCJ damage (Oheida, 2011).

The currently available studies were conducted retrospectively in adult horses during their sport horse career. Thus, it is still unclear whether specific carpal bone parameters are congenital, acquired during the post‐partum growth period, or changed with training later in life. Carpal bone measurements from young foals during their first year of growth would provide insight into whether these bone conformations are present at birth or achieved through growth. The first hypothesis was that angular carpal parameters in all three carpal regions (radiocarpal, intercarpal and carpometacarpal) would significantly change in morphology following a consistent and predictable pattern through the first 10 months of growth in foals. The second hypothesis was that there would be significant asymmetries between the left and right angular carpal parameters.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: Twenty privately owned Thoroughbred foals (12 colts and 8 fillies) that were born between January 2022 and May 2022 located in Blinded for review were included in the study. The imprinting training procedure, modified from the procedure by Miller (1991), was used to take radiographs safely without chemical restraints. All foals were kept with mares until they were weaned at 6 months. The overall health and soundness of horses were continuously monitored by experienced stud staff and checked every 2 months by the veterinarian. The foals were free to exercise themselves in the paddock (approximately 8.16 acres) throughout the study.

sec.title: Radiographs
sec.p: DP carpal radiographs of both left and right forelimbs were radiographed using Poskom Vet – 20BT Portable X‐ray from EVDS (South Grafton, NWS, Australia). All horses were confirmed to be free from any clinical signs of carpal pathologies and abnormalities before radiographs were taken. Radiographs included all carpal bones, the distal radius and proximal metacarpal bones. All DP radiographs used for analysis achieved ZDP of vertical and horizontal rotation or were evaluated to be within the acceptable rotational range (which was ±5° of vertical and/or horizontal rotation of the limb or 10° rotation in one direction), according to Oheida (2011) and Oheida et al. (2016).

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: The first radiographic images were taken within the first few weeks after birth, mostly within the first week. The second radiographic images were taken when the foals were at 2 months of age. Routine radiographs were taken every 2 months from the second radiography session. Foals were radiographed in a random order during each radiography session.

sec.title: Familiarisation with the radiographic procedure in foals
sec.p: Before the commencement of this investigation, all thoroughbred foals involved in this study went through familiarisation with image collection procedures during imprint training. This involved the desensitisation of anatomical regions including the carpus, third metacarpus and fetlock on both forelimbs. Foals were trained to stand square in the same position with minimal movement to collect high quality images that were suitable to be used for conformational measurements. For the safety of the horses and the investigator, horses that refused to follow the protocols were to be excluded from these procedures.

sec.title: Carpal parameters
sec.p: Fifteen individual angular carpal parameters and 11 relative angular carpal parameters were selected from a previous study (Oheida, 2011). Individual angular carpal parameters were measured between the specific features of the carpal bone and the reference line. Relative angular carpal parameters were formed between two perpendicular lines extended from two specific carpal features. Each feature was evaluated by drawing a transverse line placed between two specific regions of interest. The Disto‐palmar Ci – Distal Cu angle and Disto.dorsal C3 – Distal mediodorsal C4 angle were measured towards the distal side, whereas all other angular measurements were measured towards the medial side.

Individual carpal angular parameters were (Figure 1):
The radial metaphyseal angle (Ra.met angle)
The radial carpal joint angle (RCJ angle)
The middle carpal joint angle (MCJ angle)
The proximo‐dorsal angle of Cr (Prx.dor.Cr angle)
The proximo‐palmar angle of Cr (Prx.pal.Cr angle)
The disto‐palmar angle of Cr (Dis.pal.Cr angle)
The disto‐palmar angle of Ci (Dis.pal.Ci angle)
The proximal angle of Cu (Prx.Cu angle)
The distal angle of Cu (Dis.Cu angle)
The distal angle of C2 (Dis.C2 angle)
The radial facet angle of C3 (Rf.C3 angle)
The disto‐dorsal angle of C3 (Dis.dor.C3 angle)
The disto‐palmar angle of C3 (Dis.pal.C3 angle)
The distal medio‐dorsal angle of C4 (Dis.med.dor.C4 angle)
The disto‐lateral angle of C4 (Dis.lat.C4 angle)

caption.p: Individual angular carpal parameters measured from ZDP radiographs of a left carpus of a 10‐months‐old Thoroughbred foal: (1) radial metaphyseal angle; (2) radial carpal angle; (3) middle carpal angle; (4) proximo‐dorsal angle of radial carpal (Cr); (5) proximo‐palmar angle of Cr; (6) Disto‐palmar angle of Cr; (7) Disto‐palmar angle of intermediate carpal (Ci); (8) proximal angle of Cu; (9) Distal angle of Cu; (10) Distal angle of C2; (11) radial facet angle of third carpal (C3); (12) Disto‐dorsal angle of C3; (13) Disto‐palmar angle of C3; (14) Distal medio‐dorsal angle of C4 and (15) Disto‐lateral angle of C4.

sec.p:
Relative carpal angular parameters were (Figure 2):
The radial metaphyseal‐Radial carpal joint angle (Ra.met‐RCJ angle)
The radial metaphyseal‐Middle carpal joint angle (Ra.met‐MCJ angle)
The radial metaphyseal‐Radial facet C3 angle (Ra.met‐Rf.C3 angle)
The radial metaphyseal‐Distal Dorsal of C3 angle (Ra.met‐Dis.dor.C3 angle)
The radial carpal‐Middle carpal joint angle (RCJ‐MCJ angle)
The radial carpal‐Radial facet C3 or the distal radius‐radial facet angle (RCJ‐Rf.C3 angle)
The radial carpal‐Disto‐dorsal C3 angle (RCJ‐Dis.dor.C3 angle)
The middle carpal‐Disto‐dorsal C3 angle (MCJ‐Dis.dor.C3 angle)
The proximal dorsal Cr‐Proximal palmar Cr angle (Prx.dor.Cr‐Prx.pal.Cr angle)
The disto‐palmar Ci‐Distal Cu angle (Dis.pal.Ci‐Dis.Cu angle)
The disto.dorsal C3‐Distal medio‐dorsal C4 angle (Dis.dor.C3‐Dis.med.dor.C4 angle)

caption.p: Relative angular carpal parameters measured from ZDP radiographs of a left carpus of a 10‐months‐old Thoroughbred foal: (1) radial metaphyseal‐radial carpal angle; (2) radial metaphyseal‐middle carpal joint angle; (3) radial metaphyseal‐radial facet third carpal (C3) angle; (4) radial metaphyseal‐Distal Dorsal of C3 angle; (5) radial carpal‐middle carpal joint angle; (6) radial carpal‐radial facet C3 angle; (7) radial carpal‐Disto‐dorsal C3 angle; (8) middle carpal‐Disto‐dorsal C3 angle; (9) proximal dorsal radial carpal (Cr)‐proximal palmar Cr angle; (10) Disto‐palmar intermediate carpal (Ci)‐Distal Cu angle; (11) Disto.dorsal C3‐Distal medio‐dorsal C4 angle.

sec.title: Measuring carpal parameters
sec.p: Collected radiographs were imported into ImageJ (Schneider et al., 2012). Radiograph contrast was adjusted if required. Straight line and angle measuring tools were used to conduct the measurements. Each angular carpal parameter was measured three times, and the mean value of triplicate measurements was used for analysis. To decrease the risk of errors and bias, no more than 10 radiographs were measured per day. Further, two evaluators were randomly alternated to measure the radiographs.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: A linear mixed‐effects model was used to analyse the individual effects of age (per month), sex and the side of the limb, as well as the mixed effect of all three variables (age, sex and side of the limb) on carpal parameters. Bonferroni adjustment was used and p‐values less than 0.05 (<0.05) were interpreted as statistically significant. Conditional r‐squared values were also calculated to provide the proportion of variance influenced by both fixed (age and side of the limb) and random effects caused by inter‐individual variability between each horse. Both intra and inter‐rater repeatability was assessed using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC, McGraw & Wong, 1996), and inter‐rater repeatability was also reassessed using Bland and Altman limits of agreement (Bland & Altman, 1986). The normality of the data was investigated via the Shaphiro–Wilk normality test (Shaphiro & Wilk, 1965). Power simulation analysis was conducted on a linear mixed‐effects model to examine whether longitudinal measurements would provide enough power for statistical analysis (Arnold et al., 2011), and ad hoc power analysis was conducted on ICC. The Analysis was conducted in R (R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing, 2013). The range (minimum, mean, median and maximum) of statistically significant carpal parameters from each of the radiographic procedures is presented in Tables 1 and 2.

caption.p: Minimum, mean, median and maximum values of individual angular carpal parameters at each radiographic trial that showed significant changes during growth in 20 Thoroughbred foals.

fn.p: Note: First radiographic trial was conducted within the few weeks after birth (initial). From 2 months old, radiographic procedure was conducted every 2 months.
Abbreviations: C3, third carpal; Cr, radial carpal.

caption.p: Minimum, mean, median and maximum values of relative angular carpal parameters at each radiographic trial that showed significant changes during growth in 20 Thoroughbred foals.

fn.p: Note: First radiographic trial was conducted within the few weeks after birth (initial). From 2 months old, radiographic procedure was conducted every 2 months.
Abbreviations: C3, third carpal; Ci, intermediate carpal; Cr, radial carpal; MCJ, middle carpal joint; RCJ, radial carpal joint.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Blinded for review (acceptance No.: CHU22020).

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: Overall, 83 left and 74 right DP carpal radiographs (a total of 157 radiographs) within the acceptable range of alignment for measurement according to Oheida (2011) were collected for this study. One foal was excluded from the study due to an unexpected injury in the paddock which was not related to the experimental procedure. No other foals were excluded from the radiographic procedures due to any issues (behavioural and/or health). The range for statistically significant comparisons (individual and relative carpal parameters) is presented in Tables 1 and 2. The results of the power and inter‐ and intra‐rater repeatability analyses are included in the supplements. No horse exhibited congenital carpal deformities nor lameness during the 10 months of the study.

Sex did not appear to have an effect on any of the measured carpal parameters. Similarly, the combined effect (intercept) of age, side of the limb and sex did not have a significant effect on any of the measured carpal parameters. Significant associations with age and/or side of the limb were found in five individual angular carpal parameters and seven relative carpal parameters. Positive coefficients resulting from the analysis between carpal parameters and the effect of growth would mean that each of the angular carpal parameters was proximally diverging from the medial side through the 10 months of the study. The difference between the left and right carpal parameters at each time point was analysed using the right carpal parameters as a reference. Thus, a positive coefficient for the effect of side on a given carpal parameter indicates a greater proximomedial inclination in the angle measured on the left side compared to the right. All significant angular carpal parameters showed a conditional r‐squared value greater than 0.3.

Age partially explained the variance observed in four individual carpal parameters (Prx.dor.Cr, Prx.Cu, Dis.dor.C3 and Dis.pal.Ci) (Table 3). In contrast, side partially explained variations in only one individual carpal parameter (Dis.pal.Cr), with values on the left being higher than on the right (Table 4). Age also partially explained the changes in six relative carpal parameters (Prx.dor.Cr – Prx.pal.Cr, Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4, RCJ – Dis.dor.C3, MCJ – Dis.dor.C3, Ra.met – Dis.dor.C3 and Dis.pal.Ci – Dis.Cu) (Table 3). Side partially explained the variance in only one relative parameter,Ra.met – RCJ, with values on the left being higher than on the right (Table 4).

caption.p: Specific changes in individual and relative carpal parameters per month throughout the first 10 months of life in 20 Thoroughbred foals.

fn.p: Note: p‐Values were adjusted using Bonferroni correction. The coefficient provides an estimated effect of growth on each carpal parameter per month (i.e. change/month).
Abbreviations: C3, third carpal; Ci, intermediate carpal; Cr, radial carpal; MCJ, middle carpal joint; RCJ, radial carpal joint.

caption.p: The effect of side on individual and relative carpal parameters in 20 Thoroughbred foals throughout 10 months of growth.

fn.p: Note: p‐Values were adjusted using Bonferroni correction. The coefficient provides an estimate of the effect of side on each carpal parameter.
Abbreviations: Cr, radial carpal; RCJ, radial carpal joint.
A positive coefficient value indicates a greater proximomedial inclination in the angle measured on the left carpus compared to the right.

sec.p: Within the four individual carpal parameters, two presented a significant negative relationship (decrease) with the variable of age: Prx.Cu and Dis.pal.Ci. The other two angles increased with age. Age had the greatest effect on the change in Prx.Cu, which was estimated to decrease by 0.8 (±0.04) per month. The smallest change per month was found in the Dis.pal.Ci angle, which showed a mean decrease in angle of – 0.1 (±0.04) over the whole study period (Table 3). The angle that differed between the two limbs (Dis.pal.Cr) was estimated to be higher in the left limb by 0.5 (±0.2) at each time point (Table 4).

Among the relative parameters, the effect of age was the greatest in Prx.dor.Cr – Prx.pal.Cr, which was estimated to reduce by 0.80.05) per month over the 10 months of the study period. The Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4 presented the highest positive relationship with age and was estimated to increase by 0.30.04), throughout the 10 months of the study period (Table 3). The Ra.met – RCJ angle was shown to be higher in the left limb throughout the 10 months after birth (Table 4).

Not all significant individual angular parameters reflected the significant changes in the relative angular parameters that were related to them. However, significant relative angular parameters always included at least one individual parameter that was found to be significantly affected by age. Hence, the morphological changes of the relative angular parameters were interpreted based on the changes in statistically significant individual parameters that were included in the relative angular parameters. In contrast, the Ra.met – RCJ relative angle, which was found to be higher in the left limb, did not include any significant individual carpal parameter that differed between the left and right limbs. A visual presentation of specific changes in angular carpal parameters is shown in Figure 3a–c and Figure 4a,b. The results of angular carpal parameters that did not show any significant association with variables are presented in Tables S1–S4.

caption.p: Direction of change from birth (red arrows) in individual (a) and relative (b and c) angular carpal parameters that showed a statistically significant association with age as measured from ZDP radiographs of a left carpus of a 10‐month‐old Thoroughbred foal: (1) Prx.dor. radial carpal (Cr); (2) Prx.Cu; (3) Dis.dor. third carpal (C3); (4) Dis.pal. intermediate carpal (Ci); (5) Prx.dor.Cr – Prx.pal.Cr; (6) Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4; (7) radial carpal joint (RCJ) – Dis.dor.C3; (8) middle carpal joint (MCJ) – Dis.dor.C3; (9) Ra.met – Dis.dor.C3; (10) Dis.pal.Ci – Dis.Cu.

Direction of change in individual (a) and relative (b) angular carpal parameters that showed a significant difference between the left and right carpal bones as measured from ZDP radiographs of a left carpus of a 10‐month‐old Thoroughbred foal. Red arrows show the direction of change towards the right‐side carpus: (1) Dis.pal. radial carpal (Cr); (2) Ra.met – radial carpal joint (RCJ).

sec.p: Thirteen angular carpal parameters were found to be not normally distributed (Dis.pal.C3, Ra.met, Dis.lat.C4, Rf.C3, Dis.pal.Cr, Dis.pal.Ci, Prx.dor.Cr, MCJ, Ra.met – RCJ, Ra.met – Rf.C3, Ra.met – MCJ, Dis.pal.Ci – Dis.Cu and RCJ – Rf.C3). All angular carpal parameters except RCJ. Dis.Cu. Dis.C2, Rf.C3, Dis.med.dor.C4 and RCJ – Rf.C3 resulted in an intra‐rater repeatability greater than 0.7 (Tables S5 and S6). Inter‐repeatability was greater than 0.7 in all angular carpal parameters except RCJ, Dis.Cu, Dis.C2, Rf.C3, Dis.med.dor.C4 and RCJ – Rf.C3, whereas bias was less than 0.3 in all carpal parameters (Tables S7 and S8).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Radiographic measurements of angular carpal parameters as presented by Oheida et al. (2011) allowed the investigation of specific associations between carpal parameters and age as well as identifying differences between the left and right limbs. The results of the current study confirmed that certain individual and relative angular parameters changed significantly during the first 10 months of growth and showed a difference in some parameters between the left and right limbs. All three carpal regions (radiocarpal, intercarpal and carpometacarpal) were involved in significant changes during these 10 months. The significant differences between the left and right carpal parameters were observed in the radiocarpal and intercarpal regions. Although the effect of genetics is still unclear, growth (Firth et al., 2011) and the loading of the equine limb have both been shown to be related to the change in certain distal limb bone morphologies (Davies et al., 1999; Firth et al., 2005). Further, it was previously shown that the radial facet of the C3 in Thoroughbred horses was significantly proportionately wider compared to ponies, and this was assumed to be a result of anatomical adaptation, which allowed Thoroughbreds to support their greater body weight at a higher speed of exercise (Abdunnabi et al., 2012). Hence, despite the lack of measurements of body weight, changes in carpal bone shape measurements from the current study can be assumed to reflect the adaptation of the carpal bones to growth, increase in body weight and/or any locomotory activities.

sec.title: Change in the radial, intermediate and third carpal bone angular parameters with growth
sec.p: In this study, we showed specific changes in Cr, Ci and the C3 angular parameters through the first 10 months of growth.

The changes in radial and the C3 bone angular relationships were only found on the dorsal aspect, whereas the Ci bone showed a significant change on its palmar side. This could be due to different loading characteristics between the dorsal and the palmar aspect of the carpal bones. Both in vivo Safranin O staining and cadaveric loading studies confirmed that the loads on carpal bone regions were engaged selectively depending on the amount of axial load applied to the distal limb (Palmer et al., 1994). Regardless of the amount of load applied to the distal limb, the palmar aspect of both radial and intermediate facets of the C3 was shown to be constantly loaded (Palmer et al., 1994). At higher loads mimicking gallop, the dorsal aspect of the radial and intermediate facets of the C3 were intermittently loaded with a significant increase in the mean pressure on the dorsomedial to dorsolateral aspects (Palmer et al., 1994). Hence, it may be presumed that the dorsal aspect of the Cr, Ci and C3 would show a greater change in angular relationships than the palmar aspect of the carpus through growth if it was exposed to more change in the direction of loading than the palmar side. The assumed relationship between carpal bone morphology and the applied load was further supported by the finding that a 30% increase in workload in Thoroughbred foals through the first 17 months of life resulted in a significantly greater cross‐sectional C3 area (Firth et al., 2011). Furthermore, the significant individual carpal parameters measured dorsally (Prx.dor.Cr and Dis.dor.C3) presented an increase in their angle. Such an increase in the angle of Prx.dor.Cr and Dis.dor.C3 would result in a moderate upslope towards the lateral aspect of each carpal parameter. A flatter bone surface on the proximo‐dorsal Cr and disto‐dorsal C3 may provide more opportunity to re‐direct the load towards the sagittal carpal plane, which might be expected to be a response to the distribution of loading shown in the previous study (Palmer et al., 1994). The re‐direction of the load towards the centre of the carpus may result in higher stability through all three carpal regions and to its connected bones, especially with an increase in body weight and potential speed of exercise. Despite showing an opposite trend of change, a decreased angle of Dis.pal.Ci would result in a flatter bone angle. This may also minimize any re‐direction of the load towards the lateral aspect of the carpus.

sec.title: Changes in other carpal bone parameters with growth
sec.p: There have been limited studies conducted on the function and the loading of the ulnar carpal bone (Cu), and injuries in this region were found to be very rare in racehorses (McIlwraith, 2022). There has not been enough evidence to demonstrate how Cu loads under different loading conditions and growth. The intermediate facet was found to be significantly involved with an increase in loading from the dorsomedial to mediolateral aspect of the proximal carpus (Palmer et al., 1994). Hence, it could be assumed that the load received from the Ci is reduced and resolves through the intercarpal ligament attached to the C3 and the fourth carpal bone (C4) rather than through the medial aspect of the Cu. The development of a flatter Cu angle (Prx.Cu) through growth as shown in the current study may allow a better gliding movement with the Ci. This may provide greater stability to the Ci, which could result in a more distributed loading environment of the dorsomedial carpal region.

sec.title: Change in relative carpal parameters with growth
sec.p: The current study results found six relative angular carpal parameters that showed a significant association with growth (Table 3). Relative parameters represent the relative angle between two anatomical regions of interest. All relative parameters except Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4 showed an opposite trend to the significant individual parameters that were included. The Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4 was measured towards the distal side, whereas all angular measurements were measured towards the medial side. Hence, a significant increase in Dis.dor.C3 angle would also result in a significant increase in Dis.dor.C3 – Dis.med.dor.C4 if the Dis.med.dor.C4 angle remained constant. In the previous study, RCJ – Rf.C3 and Ra.met – Rf.C3 angles were found to be significantly higher in racehorses that were free from mid‐carpal joint fracture (Oheida, 2011). Our study results showed insignificant changes in these two relative angular carpal parameters in relation to growth. This may indicate that RCJ – Rf.C3 and Ra.met – Rf.C3 angles could be acquired at birth. Hence, the measurement of RCJ – Rf.C3 and Ra.met – Rf.C3 in young foals could potentially be used as an indicator to identify individuals that would be at a higher risk of early career carpal fracture. However, a comparative analysis between foals and adult angular carpal parameters would be necessary to investigate this hypothesis further. Investigation into other relative carpal parameters is very limited in adult horses; hence, the relationships between relative carpal parameters, pathology, injuries and performance are yet to be elucidated. However, the current study provides evidence of an adaptation of carpal bones during growth and free exercise, which may be associated with increasing loads from the increasing body size. Further investigation involving a comparative analysis between adult horses and foals may help to define normal development in equine carpal bones during growth. Moreover, such an investigation may provide the opportunity to identify carpal measurements of potential use as an indicator of the likelihood of carpal injuries occurring during an individual horse's racing career.

sec.title: The difference between the left and right angular carpal bone parameters
sec.p: As horses involved in this study were not allocated to any specific exercise regimes, the results may provide further insight into laterality and other genetic variables affecting bone adaptation. It was found that Dis.pal.Cr angles were significantly higher on the left limb. Further, the significant relative carpal parameter that showed a significant difference between the left and right included Ra.met (relative angular parameter: Ra.met – RCJ). The trend of equine carpal research has always been focused on the C3 and its related bone facets. This seems likely to be due to its function as a primary load bearer and the evidence of a high prevalence of C3 injuries. The result of the current study showed a more proximally inclined Ra.met or RCJ angle of the medial side on the left limb compared to the right. The angulation of Ra.met or RCJ may influence the direction of the load towards the distal radius and to the proximal Cr, which could then affect the overall distribution of the load on the proximal carpal bones. A steeper Ra.met and RCJ angle may allow the load to be re‐directed more towards the radial and intermediate facet of C3, which was found to be a major load bearer in adult horses (Palmer et al., 1994). Further, a lower Ra.met and RCJ angle in adult Thoroughbred racehorses was found to be significantly associated with early career MCJ damage and a shorter racing career (Oheida, 2011). The Ra.met – RCJ angle provides a relative angle between the radial metaphysis and radiocarpal bone angle. A flatter Ra.met angle relative to RCJ could result in less load re‐direction towards the major loading structures, which may result in a relatively unstable loading environment in the right compared to the left limb. This assumption may also be related to the higher incidence of right C3 and Cr injuries and fractures in adult horses (Schneider et al., 1988; Wyburn & Goulden, 1974). A steeper Dis.pal.Cr on the left limb could be an adaptation reflecting a higher exposure of the load towards the medial aspect of the carpus from a steeper Ra.met or RCJ angle at early stages of growth. However, as steeper angles were found in the left Ra.met and Dis.pal.Cr consistently at each time point, this asymmetry may have been inherited genetically and maintained through growth. The left forelimb is known to have a greater incidence of damage compared to the right regardless of the direction of racing (Buckingham & Jeffcott, 1990; Sun et al., 2019) even though the right carpus seems to be more easily fractured (Schneider et al., 1988; Wyburn & Goulden, 1974). However, the findings of the current study suggest that there may be differences in the stability and/or loading of the carpus between the two front limbs, which may help explain the differences in the likelihood of damage distally and within the carpus.

sec.title: Limitation
sec.p: Acquisition of 2D radiographs and measurement of 3D structures on them requires practice to accurately identify the landmarks and produce repeatable and reliable data. In a cadaveric study, Ra.met – RCJ was the only parameter that showed less than excellent repeatability (Oheida, 2011). A subsequent part of that study included archival live horse radiographs so the slightly lower repeatability in that part of their study compared to the current study may be due to variations in the radiographic setup or other differences between adults and foals. In the current study, the distance between the carpus and the X‐ray machine was kept constant, and each radiograph was immediately checked to ensure the alignment and to locate the anatomical landmarks. In case of unsatisfactory images, the process was repeated as required. The radiographs included a calibrating element of known length, which further ensured the reliability of each measurement. The current study included just 20 foals from one stud, so all the foals were in similar conditions and each foal acted as their own control for the effects of growth. Any environmental conditions that could potentially affect the way the bones change with growth may have affected these results. A larger sample size from a wider variety of pedigrees is required to establish the normal range for each of the parameters measured in this study and to increase the generalizability of the current study results.

The results of our study showed conditional r‐squared values of 0.8 or more in one individual carpal parameter in relation to growth (Prx.Cu) and all the carpal parameters that showed a significant difference between the left and right limbs (Tables 3 and 4). The r‐squared values of the remaining significant carpal parameters were lower than 0.8 (Table 3). Traditionally, effect sizes that are larger than 0.8 are considered to be great enough to be clinically relevant. However, it was recently suggested that there should be no rule for interpreting the strength of r‐squared values in their application to clinical relevance or significance (Hamilton et al., 2015). In human clinical studies including a comparative analysis of radiographic parameters of the hip joint, the reported r‐squared values were between 0.2 and 0.4 (Weber et al., 2015). It is likely that our study would have less control over variables than in human studies, especially as the foals in our study were not sedated. The range of r‐squared values from our studies was within the range of previously reported r‐squared values from that human radiographic study (Weber et al., 2015). However, the clinical significance of r‐squared values remains to be answered and should be interpreted with caution.

Despite having high power in all statistically significant carpal parameters (>80, except Ra.met – RCJ), other statistically insignificant carpal parameters showed a relatively low degree of power. This may be due to the small sample size, which has been established to be a main limitation in equine research in vivo (Egan et al., 2019). A small sample size may also explain the non‐normal distribution of the raw data and residuals with respect to some of the parameters measured. That said, the linear mixed‐effects model has previously been confirmed to be used with confidence even if the distribution of residuals violated the distributional assumption (Schielzeth et al., 2020). However, the violation of the distributional assumption may result in increased variability in estimates (Schielzeth et al., 2020).

Due to the restricted availability of resources, the effect of body weight was excluded from this analysis. Hence, further investigation is required to investigate the effect of body weight on angular carpal parameters, as an increase in body weight with growth may influence the load applied to the carpus through the first 10 months of growth.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: The results from the present study confirmed that several specific individual and relative carpal parameters measured routinely over 10 months from birth showed a significant difference over time and between the left and right limbs. The steeper carpal bone angles achieved proximally may contribute to stabilizing the limb by redirecting the load more medially through the carpus and to its proximal and distal bones. However, further study would be required to clarify the association between changes in specific carpal parameters observed in this investigation, with carpal pathology, and risk of injury.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: All listed authors fulfilled the minimum requirements defined by the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE). Detailed descriptions of each author's contributions are as follows: Jung K. Son and Simon Bailey Substantial contributions to the conception and design of the work. Major contribution in drafting the manuscript which includes data analysis and interpretation. Jihyun Kim Substantial contribution to data acquisition. Major contribution in reviewing the draft of the manuscript. Patroys De Paz Substantial contribution to data analysis and interpretation. Major contribution in reviewing the statistical outcome and drafting of the manuscript. Reza Sanaei Substantial contribution in reviewing the script; statistical outcome and drafting of the manuscript. Major contribution in reviewing and declaring the final approval of the version to be published. Seungho Ryu Substantial contribution to the data acquisition. Major contribution in reviewing and declaring the final approval of the version to be published. Helen M.S. Davies Substantial contributions to the conception and design of work. Major contribution in reviewing and declaring the final approval of the version to be published.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: None

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of Jeju Halla University (acceptance No.: CHU22020).

sec.title: OWNER INFORMED CONSENT
sec.p: Owner gave informed consent for their horses’ inclusion in this study.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1539.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: The authors would like to acknowledge Dr.Aiman Oheida for academic discussions regarding the study outcome.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are openly available in “flagshare” at http://doi.org/10.26188/23937678 and http://doi.org/10.26188/23937672.
